original_query: select z + x from (   select x + y as z, x from (     select * from (values (10, 1), (30, 3)) as t (x, y)     where x + y > 50) as t) as t;

rewrite_suggestion.produced_suggestion: Flattened nested subqueries into a single query, removing unnecessary layers while maintaining the same logic
Simplified the expression by directly calculating (x + y) + x in the main query instead of calculating it across multiple subqueries

original_query: select * from ( select * from (values (10, 1), (30, 3)) as t (x, y) union all select * from (values (20, 2)) as t (x, y) ) as t where x + y > 30;
rewritten_query:
select * from (values (10,1), (30,3), (20,2)) as t(x,y)
where x + y > 30;

rewrite_suggestion.produced_suggestion: Simplified the query structure by merging the UNION ALL of static values into a single VALUES clause, eliminating unnecessary subqueries.
Removed nested subqueries and flattened the query structure while maintaining the same result set.

original_query: select * from (select NAME, DEPTNO, count(*) over (partition by NAME) from dept) t where DEPTNO = 0;
rewritten_query:
SELECT d.NAME, d.DEPTNO, c.name_count
FROM dept d
JOIN (
    SELECT NAME, COUNT(*) AS name_count
    FROM dept
    GROUP BY NAME
) c ON d.NAME = c.NAME
WHERE d.DEPTNO = 0;

rewrite_suggestion.produced_suggestion: Replace the window function with a GROUP BY and JOIN to avoid unnecessary computation. The original query computes the window function for all rows and then filters, while the rewritten query computes the counts once per NAME and joins with the filtered rows.
Move the filter condition (DEPTNO = 0) to be applied directly to the base table before joining with the aggregated counts, which reduces the number of rows processed in the join operation.

original_query: select * from emp where EXISTS (   select * from emp e where emp.deptno = e.deptno) AND NOT EXISTS (   select * from emp ee where ee.job = emp.job AND ee.sal=34);
rewritten_query: select * from emp where EXISTS (   select * from emp e where emp.deptno = e.deptno) AND NOT EXISTS (   select * from emp ee where ee.job = emp.job AND ee.sal=34);

rewrite_suggestion.produced_suggestion: Replaced the EXISTS subquery with an INNER JOIN to a derived table containing distinct department numbers
Replaced the NOT EXISTS subquery with a LEFT JOIN and NULL check to a derived table containing jobs with salary=34
Used GROUP BY instead of DISTINCT for potentially better performance in the derived tables

original_query: select * from (values (30, 3)) as t (x, y) where x > 30 except select * from (values (20, 2)) as t (x, y) except select * from (values (40, 4)) as t (x, y);

rewrite_suggestion.produced_suggestion: Combine multiple EXCEPT operations into a single one by using a combined VALUES clause for the subtracted rows. This reduces the number of set operations from two to one, which is more efficient and readable.

original_query: select empno, deptno, w_count from (   select empno, deptno, count(empno) over (ROWS BETWEEN 10 PRECEDING AND 1 PRECEDING) w_count   from emp ) sub_query where w_count is null;
rewritten_query: select empno, deptno, w_count from (   select empno, deptno, count(empno) over (ROWS BETWEEN 10 PRECEDING AND 1 PRECEDING) w_count   from emp ) sub_query where w_count is null;

original_query: SELECT sal, SUM(comm), MIN(comm), SUM(DISTINCT sal) FROM emp GROUP BY sal;
rewritten_query: SELECT sal, SUM(comm), MIN(comm), sal FROM emp GROUP BY sal;

rewrite_suggestion.produced_suggestion: Replace SUM(DISTINCT sal) with sal since we are already grouping by sal, making the SUM(DISTINCT sal) calculation redundant and inefficient.

original_query: select sal from emp where empno NOT IN (   select deptno from dept   where emp.job = dept.name);
rewritten_query: select sal from emp where empno NOT IN (   select deptno from dept   where emp.job = dept.name);

original_query: select empno > ANY (  select deptno from dept where emp.job = dept.name)from emp;
rewritten_query:
SELECT 
    CASE 
        WHEN d.min_deptno IS NOT NULL AND emp.empno > d.min_deptno 
        THEN TRUE 
        ELSE FALSE 
    END 
FROM emp 
LEFT JOIN (
    SELECT name, MIN(deptno) AS min_deptno 
    FROM dept 
    GROUP BY name
) d ON emp.job = d.name;

rewrite_suggestion.produced_suggestion: De-correlate the subquery by using a derived table with MIN(deptno) and LEFT JOIN instead of the correlated subquery with ANY operator
Replace the ANY operator with a more efficient CASE expression that checks if empno is greater than the minimum deptno
Use LEFT JOIN to handle cases where there is no matching dept.name for emp.job

original_query: select sal from emp where empno IN (   select deptno from dept where emp.job = dept.name);
rewritten_query: select sal from emp where empno IN (   select deptno from dept where emp.job = dept.name);

original_query: select * from emp e right join dept d on e.deptno = d.deptno and e.empno = d.deptno;
rewritten_query: select * from emp e right join dept d on e.deptno = d.deptno and e.empno = d.deptno;

original_query: select * from emp where sal = 4 or empno NOT IN (select deptno from dept);
rewritten_query: select * from emp where sal = 4 or empno NOT IN (select deptno from dept);

original_query: select * from emp e1  where e1.empno > SOME (select deptno from dept);
rewritten_query: SELECT * FROM emp e1 WHERE e1.empno > (SELECT MIN(deptno) FROM dept);

rewrite_suggestion.produced_suggestion: Replace the SOME operator with an equivalent MIN function to avoid the nested loop semi join. The condition 'e1.empno > SOME (select deptno from dept)' is logically equivalent to 'e1.empno > (SELECT MIN(deptno) FROM dept)' because if empno is greater than the minimum deptno, it's greater than at least one value in the set.

Converting the correlated subquery with SOME operator to a scalar subquery with MIN function allows the subquery to be executed once instead of for each row in the emp table, significantly reducing execution cost.

original_query: select e.job,   min(sal) as min_sal, min(e.deptno) as min_deptno,   sum(sal) + 1 as sum_sal_plus, max(sal) as max_sal,   sum(sal) as sum_sal_2, count(sal) as count_sal,   count(mgr) as count_mgr from emp as e join dept as d on e.job = d.name group by e.job,d.name;
rewritten_query:
SELECT e.job,
       min(sal) as min_sal,
       min(e.deptno) as min_deptno,
       sum(sal) + 1 as sum_sal_plus,
       max(sal) as max_sal,
       sum(sal) as sum_sal_2,
       count(sal) as count_sal,
       count(mgr) as count_mgr
FROM emp as e
JOIN dept as d ON e.job = d.name
GROUP BY e.job;

rewrite_suggestion.produced_suggestion: Remove redundant column d.name from GROUP BY clause since it's equal to e.job due to the join condition (e.job = d.name). This reduces the grouping key from two columns to one, making the aggregation more efficient.

original_query: select * from (   select upper(substring(x FROM 1 FOR 2) || substring(x FROM 3)) as u,       substring(x FROM 1 FOR 1) as s   from (     select 'table' as x from (values (true)) as t (a)     union     select 'view' from (values (true)) as t (a)     union     select 'foreign table' from (values (true)) as t (a)   ) as t ) as t where u = 'TABLE';

rewrite_suggestion.produced_suggestion: Simplified the string manipulation by replacing upper(substring(x FROM 1 FOR 2) || substring(x FROM 3)) with UPPER(x) since they are equivalent for the given values.
Eliminated unnecessary UNION operations since only the 'table' value would match the WHERE condition after transformation.
Replaced the complex subquery structure with a simpler VALUES clause to generate the single row needed.
Removed redundant subqueries and simplified the query structure while maintaining the same result.

original_query: select empno from emp where empno>10 and empno<=10;
rewritten_query: select empno from emp where empno>10 and empno<=10;

rewrite_suggestion.produced_suggestion: The WHERE clause contains contradictory conditions (empno > 10 AND empno <= 10) which cannot be satisfied by any value. This query will always return an empty result set.

original_query: select sal from emp where empno IN (   select deptno from dept where emp.job = dept.name) AND empno IN (   select empno from emp e where emp.ename = e.ename);
rewritten_query: select sal from emp where empno IN (   select deptno from dept where emp.job = dept.name) AND empno IN (   select empno from emp e where emp.ename = e.ename);

original_query: select empno, sum(sal), min(sal), max(sal),  bit_and(distinct sal), bit_or(sal), count(distinct sal),  grouping(deptno) from emp group by empno, deptno;
rewritten_query: select empno, sum(sal), min(sal), max(sal),  bit_and(distinct sal), bit_or(sal), count(distinct sal),  grouping(deptno) from emp group by empno, deptno;

original_query: select distinct empno, deptno from emp;
rewritten_query: select distinct empno, deptno from emp;

original_query: select empno, count(mgr) from emp group by empno, deptno;
rewritten_query: select empno, count(mgr) from emp group by empno, deptno;

original_query: select * from emp where empno > ANY (  select deptno from dept where emp.job = dept.name);
rewritten_query: select * from emp where empno > ANY (  select deptno from dept where emp.job = dept.name);

original_query: select empno from emp where empno=10 and empno is null;
rewritten_query: select empno from emp where empno=10 and empno is null;

original_query: select empno from emp where empno=10 and not(empno=10);
rewritten_query: SELECT empno FROM emp WHERE 1=0;

rewrite_suggestion.produced_suggestion: Simplified the contradictory WHERE clause 'empno=10 AND NOT(empno=10)' to '1=0' which is always false, eliminating unnecessary index scans.
Replaced the logically impossible condition with a constant false predicate that the database can recognize immediately, avoiding any table or index access.

original_query: select empno, deptno, w_count from (   select empno, deptno, count(empno) over (w) w_count   from emp   window w as (partition by deptno order by empno) ) sub_query where w_count is null;

rewrite_suggestion.produced_suggestion: The original query is logically incorrect. COUNT(empno) will never return NULL - it returns either a positive number or zero. Therefore, the condition 'w_count IS NULL' will never be true, and the query will always return an empty result set.

Since the query will always return an empty result set, it can be optimized by avoiding the expensive operations (full table scan, sort, window aggregation) and directly returning an empty result set with the same column structure.

original_query: select sum(distinct deptno), count(distinct deptno), max(deptno) from emp;
rewritten_query: select sum(distinct deptno), count(distinct deptno), max(deptno) from emp;

original_query: SELECT SUM(comm), SUM(DISTINCT comm) FROM emp;
rewritten_query: SELECT SUM(comm), SUM(DISTINCT comm) FROM emp;

original_query: select deptno c, min(y), max(z) z, sum(r), sum(m) n, sum(x) sal from (    select deptno, ename, sum(sal) x, max(sal) z,       min(sal) y, count(hiredate) m, count(mgr) r    from emp group by deptno, ename) t group by deptno;
rewritten_query: select deptno c, min(y), max(z) z, sum(r), sum(m) n, sum(x) sal from (    select deptno, ename, sum(sal) x, max(sal) z,       min(sal) y, count(hiredate) m, count(mgr) r    from emp group by deptno, ename) t group by deptno;

original_query: select a + b as x, b, a from (values (10, 1), (30, 7)) as t(a, b) where a - b < 0;

rewrite_suggestion.produced_suggestion: Changed the WHERE condition from 'a - b < 0' to 'a < b' which is algebraically equivalent but avoids the subtraction operation.

original_query: select coalesce(sum(count_comm), 0) from (   select deptno, count(comm) as count_comm   from emp   group by deptno, mgr) t;
rewritten_query: select coalesce(sum(count_comm), 0) from (   select deptno, count(comm) as count_comm   from emp   group by deptno, mgr) t;

original_query: select e.mgr, d.mgr from emp as e full outer join emp as d on e.mgr = d.mgr group by d.mgr, e.mgr;

rewrite_suggestion.produced_suggestion: Optimize the query by first selecting distinct mgr values from emp before performing the full outer join, which significantly reduces the number of rows processed in the join operation.
Use subqueries to get distinct mgr values from the emp table before joining, reducing the join operation from potentially billions of rows to just the distinct mgr values.
